content.text: Multiples are numbers obtained by multiplying a given number by 1, 2, 3, and so on. Common multiples are numbers that appear in the multiple lists of two or more numbers.

content.title: Idli-Vada Game
content.conclusion: The first 'idli-vada' number is 15, which is the smallest common multiple of 3 and 5.

content.title: Example-1
content.question: If the idli-vada game is played till 90, how many times will 'idli-vada' (common multiples of 3 and 5) be said?
content.solution:
1. Common multiples of 3 and 5 are 15, 30, 45, 60, 75, 90.
2. Counting them, we get 6 times.

content.text: Factors (or divisors) are numbers that divide a given number exactly, leaving no remainder. A number can have multiple factors.

content.title: Perfect Number
content.text: A number for which the sum of all its factors is equal to twice the number is called a perfect number.

content.title: Example-2
content.question: Find the factors of 20 and 28 and identify their common factors.

content.title: Definitions
items: **Prime Numbers**: Numbers with exactly two factors (1 and the number itself). Examples: 2, 3, 5, 7.
**Composite Numbers**: Numbers with more than two factors. Examples: 4, 6, 8, 9.
**Note**: 1 is neither prime nor composite. 2 is the only even prime number.

content.title: Sieve of Eratosthenes
procedure: Circle 2 and cross out all its multiples.
Continue for the next uncrossed number until all are processed.
content.conclusion: The circled numbers are all prime numbers up to 100.

content.title: Example-3
content.question: Are 23 and 51 prime or composite?
content.solution:
1. 23 has factors 1 and 23 only. It is **prime**.
2. 51 has factors 1, 3, 17, 51 ($3 \times 17 = 51$). It has more than two factors, so it is **composite**.

content.text: Two numbers are said to be co-prime to each other if they have no common factor other than 1.

content.title: Example-4
content.question: Are 18 and 35 co-prime?
content.solution:
1. Factors of 18: 1, 2, 3, 6, 9, 18.
2. Factors of 35: 1, 5, 7, 35.
3. Common factor is only 1. Therefore, they are **co-prime**.

content.text: Every number greater than 1 can be expressed as a product of prime numbers. This unique expression (ignoring the order) is called prime factorisation.

content.title: Example-5
content.question: Find the prime factorisation of 36.
content.solution:
36 can be broken down as:
$36 = 2 \times 18$
$= 2 \times 2 \times 9$
$= 2 \times 2 \times 3 \times 3$
Prime factorisation: $2 \times 2 \times 3 \times 3$.

content.title: Example-6
content.question: Is 168 divisible by 12 using prime factorisation?
content.solution:
1. Prime factorisation of 168: $2 \times 2 \times 2 \times 3 \times 7$
2. Prime factorisation of 12: $2 \times 2 \times 3$
3. Since $2, 2, 3$ are all present in 168's factors, 168 is divisible by 12.

content.title: Test Rules
items: **By 4**: Number formed by the last two digits must be divisible by 4.
**By 8**: Number formed by the last three digits must be divisible by 8.

content.title: Example-7
content.question: Is 8536 divisible by 4?
content.solution:
1. Last two digits are 36.
2. Since 36 is divisible by 4 ($4 \times 9 = 36$), the entire number 8536 is divisible by 4.

content.title: Example-8
content.question: Identify which of these are leap years: 2024, 2100, 2400.
content.solution:
1. 2024 is a multiple of 4, so it is a **leap year**.
2. 2100 is divisible by 100 but not 400, so it is **not a leap year**.
3. 2400 is divisible by both 100 and 400, so it is a **leap year**.

glossary.definition: The product of a given number and any whole number.
A number that divides another number exactly without leaving a remainder.
A number that has exactly two factors: 1 and itself.
A number that has more than two factors.
Two numbers whose only common factor is 1.
A number where the sum of its factors (excluding the number itself) equals the number, or sum of all factors equals twice the number.

summary_points: Factors are divisors, while multiples are products of numbers.
Prime numbers have only two factors, whereas composite numbers have more.
The Sieve of Eratosthenes is a classic systematic method for finding all prime numbers in a range.
Co-prime numbers share no common factor except 1, which affects their least common multiples.
Prime factorisation is the unique way of breaking down composite numbers into prime building blocks.
Divisibility tests for 2, 4, 5, 8, and 10 rely on observing patterns in the last few digits of a number.
